name: B.S. in Business and Information Systems

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The eight-term plan interleaves a coherent IS spine (CS 100 -> IS 117 -> IS 218 -> IS 331 database; IS 265 -> IS 344 -> IS 465 Advanced IS; IS 247/IS 375 UX; IS 390 requirements analysis; IS 455 IS management & business processes) with a well-sequenced business core (ECON 201, ACCT 117, FIN 218, FIN 315, MGMT 216 Business Data Analytics, MRKT 330, MGMT 391, HRM 301). Term loads are steady at 12-16 credits and culminate in an IS 491/IT 491 capstone, so difficulty ramps smoothly. The main weakness is that the two disciplines run largely in parallel with only IS 344/IS 455/MGMT 216 explicitly bridging them.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an explicit integrative course or capstone theme that forces the business and IS threads together (e.g., an analytics-for-business-decisions project spanning IS 465 and MGMT 216) so the two spines converge rather than run side by side.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is sensible: deepestPrereqChain is 7 (IS 465 Advanced IS, a terminal course gated on a statistical GER plus IS 265/IS 344 and IS 331), which is appropriate depth for a senior course and not over-constraining, while foundational courses sit at shallow depths. missingCourseCount is 4 and every dangling reference is peripheral -- GER 200 and GER 300 are general-education level placeholders, and YWCC 207/YWCC 307 are the one-credit professional-development seminars that appear in the plan but are not catalogued as course entries.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the four dangling references by linking YWCC 207/YWCC 307 to their catalog entries and re-tagging GER 200/GER 300 as requirement categories rather than course codes.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: The program data contains no program-level learning outcomes, educational objectives, or program overview. A prospective student can only infer the intended competencies by reading the course list, with no explicit statement of what a Business & IS graduate will know or be able to do -- a genuine gap for a catalog page.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 5-8 program-level learning outcomes spanning IS analysis/design, database and web systems, business analytics, and professional communication, alongside a short program overview.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: Credit accounting reconciles cleanly: statedTotalCredits is 120, summedTermCredits is 120, and creditReconcileDelta is 0.0. The split is transparent and balanced across general education, a substantial business core, and the IS core, with roughly five general-elective slots providing flexibility. The only caveat is that the business load is heavy enough that quantitative/technical IS depth (programming, analytics) competes for limited room.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a one-line credit summary (gen-ed vs. business core vs. IS core vs. electives) so the ~120-credit split is visible at a glance and the business/IS balance is explicit.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are clearly labeled and most rows carry code, title, and credits, but several presentation defects remain: general-education rows are split and carry null credits (e.g., 'Science Literacy GER', 'History and Humanities GER 200 level'), alternative options render as separate null-code lines ('or ACCT 115 or Fundamentals of Financial Accounting', 'or COM 313 or Technical Writing'), and multiple titles are abbreviated ('Principles Of Fin Accountng', 'IS Mgmt & Business Processes', 'Computing & Effective Com').
dimensions.suggestion: Merge each split GER row and each 'or ...' alternative into a single readable line, and restore full course titles in place of the truncated registrar abbreviations.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The program reflects real business-IS practice via database design (IS 331), e-commerce technology (IT 310), business data analytics (MGMT 216), UX (IS 247/IS 375), and IS management (IS 455/IS 465). However, for a contemporary Business & IS degree the analytics footprint is thin (essentially MGMT 216 plus the statistics touched in IS 465), there is no information-security/governance course, no enterprise-systems/ERP exposure, and the only programming is web development -- leaving a graduate short of the Python/SQL analytics fluency employers now expect.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a required data-analytics course using Python/SQL and a BI visualization tool (Tableau/Power BI), and a business information-security & governance elective, to align with current analyst and IS-management hiring.

descriptionQuality.justification: All 33 sampled courses carry present, specific descriptions with no missing or placeholder entries; excerpts convey real topical content (e.g., IS 218 'critical, hands-on introduction to the design of Web-based Information Systems', IS 331 databases for analysis/decision-making, IS 465 data analysis and statistics from an IS perspective). A few registrar titles are terse, but the descriptive text itself is informative rather than thin.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated with explicit grade gates ('with a grade of C or better') and sensible boolean alternatives (IS 218: '(IS 117 or IT 202) and (CS 100, CS 113, or CS 115)'; IS 465 lists an explicit statistical-GER set). Intro courses correctly show no prerequisite. Minor blemishes -- GER-set placeholders inside prerequisite text (IS 350) and slight phrasing variance ('C or better' vs 'C or higher') -- keep it from a 5.

missingTopics.topic: Hands-on business/data analytics (Python, SQL, BI dashboards)
missingTopics.rationale: Analytics is limited to MGMT 216 and the statistics in IS 465; a modern Business & IS graduate needs applied Python/SQL and a dashboarding tool -- add a dedicated analytics course rather than relying on a single survey.

missingTopics.topic: Information security, risk & IT governance (GRC, SOX/GDPR)
missingTopics.rationale: No course addresses security or compliance despite IT 310 e-commerce and IS 455 business processes; business IS professionals are routinely responsible for data-protection and regulatory risk.

missingTopics.topic: Enterprise systems / ERP and process integration
missingTopics.rationale: IS 455 covers business processes conceptually but there is no hands-on ERP (SAP/Oracle) or process-integration experience that business-IS roles rely on.
IE 492 Engineering Management is generic; a dedicated Agile/PM course would match how IS projects (IS 390 requirements, IS 491 capstone) are actually delivered in industry.
Given the FIN/ACCT/MRKT reporting context, students would benefit from dimensional modeling and warehouse/BI pipeline concepts beyond the transactional focus of IS 331.

emergingTrends.trend: Generative AI for business productivity and analytics
emergingTrends.rationale: LLM copilots are reshaping accounting, marketing, and analyst workflows this program feeds; an 'AI for business' elective would build on MGMT 216 and IS 465.
Business systems increasingly run on Salesforce/ServiceNow/Power Platform; IS 344/IS 455 could be modernized to teach configuration and low-code app building.

emergingTrends.trend: Process mining and robotic process automation (RPA)
emergingTrends.rationale: IS 455 business-process content is a natural home for process mining and RPA, which are now central to operational-efficiency roles.
With FIN 218/FIN 315 and IT 310 e-commerce already present, the program is well positioned to add a FinTech elective covering digital payments and platform finance.

emergingTrends.trend: Data privacy and responsible data governance
emergingTrends.rationale: Building on IS 350 ethics, GDPR/CCPA-style governance is now a core expectation for anyone managing business information systems.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program-level learning outcomes and a short overview; the data currently contains none.
Add a required applied-analytics course (Python/SQL + BI dashboards) to deepen the thin analytics footprint beyond MGMT 216.
Introduce an information-security/IT-governance course and an enterprise-systems/ERP experience to close the two largest industry gaps.
Add an Agile/project-management course to support IS 390 and the IS 491 capstone with real delivery practice.
Clean the plan-of-study presentation: merge split GER and 'or ...' rows and restore truncated course titles.
